ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Tom Yum Seafood Noodle Soup; closest parent 'noodle soup food product' (FOODON_00002572) used as the nearest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FHR (Noodle soup) is the closest parent category; no specific Tom Yum seafood noodle code exists in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for Tom Yum Seafood Noodle Soup as a specific food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Tom Yum Seafood Noodle Soup is approximately 450 g, comprising a bowl of spicy-sour broth with mixed seafood and noodles.

nutrient_source.title: Tom Yum Seafood Noodle Soup — estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (Shrimp cooked SR Legacy ID 175180; Squid cooked SR Legacy ID 175185; Rice noodles cooked SR Legacy ID 169726; Tom Yum broth estimated from standard Thai soup base components) and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia (2nd edition) for Thai soup broth reference values.
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Tom Yum Seafood Noodle Soup as a composite dish. Values are estimated by weighted component assembly: approximately 35% rice noodles (cooked), 30% mixed seafood (prawns, squid, fish), and 35% tom yum broth. USDA SR Legacy used for seafood and noodle components; FAO/INFOODS SEA used for Thai broth reference. Sodium is elevated due to fish sauce and nam prik pao in the broth. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — rice noodles and mixed seafood soup analogous estimate; rice noodles GI ~53 (Atkinson et al., 2021); broth and seafood components contribute negligible glycemic load, reducing composite GI.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Tom Yum Seafood Noodle Soup. GI estimated at 40 based on the dominant carbohydrate source being rice noodles (cooked, GI ~53 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international tables). The composite GI is reduced below the rice noodle baseline due to the high water content of the broth (~35% of dish weight), the protein and fat from seafood components (which slow gastric emptying), and the acidic lime juice in the tom yum broth (acidity lowers GI). Glycemic load per 450 g serving calculated as: (40 × 38.25 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 15.3 g GL.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and nam prik pao
hypertension.guidance: A single 450 g serving provides approximately 1890 mg sodium (45% of the 4200 mg WHO upper daily limit), primarily from fish sauce and roasted chilli paste; individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce or consume a smaller portion to limit sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice noodles, partially offset by protein, fat, and broth acidity
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated glycemic index of 40 and glycemic load of approximately 15 per serving make this dish moderately suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; portion control of the noodle component and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables can further reduce postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate cholesterol from mixed seafood, partially offset by low saturated fat and omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (approximately 1890 mg per serving) is the primary cardiovascular concern; however, the low saturated fat content (1.8 g per 100 g) and the presence of omega-3-rich seafood are beneficial, and individuals with cardiovascular disease should focus on sodium reduction strategies when consuming this dish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tom Yum Seafood Noodle Soup is a classic Thai dish combining the bold, aromatic flavours of tom yum broth with mixed fresh seafood — typically prawns, squid, and fish — served over rice noodles. The broth is prepared by simmering lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, fresh chilli, and mushrooms in a stock base, then seasoned with fish sauce, lime juice, and nam prik pao (roasted chilli paste) to achieve the characteristic spicy-sour-savoury balance. The dish is served hot in a deep bowl, with the noodles absorbing the intensely flavoured broth. Texturally, the soup offers tender seafood, soft noodles, and a light but richly aromatic liquid. In Singapore, this dish is commonly available at Thai restaurants and food courts, often listed as 'Tom Yum Noodle' or 'Seafood Tom Yum Noodle'. Nutritionally, the dish is moderate in energy (approximately 72 kcal per 100 g or 324 kcal per 450 g serving), relatively high in protein from the seafood component (approximately 6.2 g per 100 g), low in saturated fat, and high in sodium due to fish sauce and chilli paste. The estimated glycemic index is 40 (low), making it a reasonable carbohydrate choice for glycaemic management when portion size is controlled. The primary clinical concern is sodium content, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is dairy-free and generally halal when prepared with halal-certified seafood and condiments.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Tom Yum Seafood Noodle Soup as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component assembly using USDA SR Legacy data for seafood and rice noodle components and FAO/INFOODS SEA reference values for Thai soup broth. GI is an analogous estimate based on the dominant carbohydrate source (rice noodles) with documented adjustments for broth dilution, protein/fat co-ingestion, and broth acidity. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated; no direct approved-source composite entry was found for this dish. Values should be verified against a direct laboratory analysis or an approved regional food composition entry if one becomes available. 2. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported for this dish type in the component sources used (USDA SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA). 3. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources for this dish. 4. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA reference used for the broth component; USDA SR Legacy values for individual seafood items vary and a reliable composite estimate cannot be made. 5. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in the component sources — no industrially produced trans fats are expected in this traditionally prepared dish. 6. The input food_name 'T1 Seafood Caribbean Noodle' appears to be a restaurant menu label (Saap Saap Thai, Funan, Singapore); the canonical food name has been corrected to 'Tom Yum Seafood Noodle Soup' based on the dish description referencing Tom Yum soup with seafood and noodles. 7. GI is estimated at 40 based on rice noodle GI (~53) adjusted downward for broth dilution, protein/fat co-ingestion, and lime juice acidity per University of Sydney GI Database analogous methodology. 8. Sodium content is high due to fish sauce and nam prik pao; this is the primary clinical concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management.
